skill.name: image-inpainting
skill.description: RunComfy image inpainting skill for masked region edits, object cleanup, background replacement, and description-based fallback edits.
skill.summary: Edit masked image regions with RunComfy inpainting models.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static command and weak-crypto alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, model names, and prose. Real risks remain because the skill intentionally invokes the runcomfy CLI, uploads image and mask URLs to RunComfy services, and documents local token storage.

medium_findings.title: Intentional External CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill grants Bash access for runcomfy commands and includes multiple runcomfy run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it creates a command execution boundary that depends on CLI behavior and user-supplied inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and command examples directly show CLI execution. The scope is limited to runcomfy, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Network Processing of User Images
medium_findings.description: The skill sends source image and mask URLs to RunComfy model APIs for processing. This can expose private images or sensitive visual content to a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The schema and command examples require image URLs, and the skill states the CLI posts to the Model API. The privacy risk is inherent to the documented workflow.

medium_findings.title: Local API Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login stores an API token under the user configuration directory. The documented 0600 mode reduces exposure, but compromised local access could still recover the token.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permissions are explicitly documented. The permission mode is appropriate, so the concern is credential handling rather than evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives from Markdown Text
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick and weak cryptography alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code formatting, model names, table text, and ordinary words. No Ruby code, cryptographic API, or dynamic shell construction was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review shows Markdown prose and inline code markers, not executable Ruby or cryptographic operations. The file is documentation with CLI examples.

low_findings.title: Benign Hardcoded Documentation and Service URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy documentation, model catalog pages, example CDN placeholders, and declared service endpoints. They are expected for a hosted image-processing skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and related to the advertised service. No evidence found of hidden endpoints, unrelated domains, or covert telemetry.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Pattern for runcomfy
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill permits Bash(runcomfy *) so agents can execute the RunComfy CLI. This should stay limited to runcomfy and should not be expanded to arbitrary shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool scope is directly declared. The restriction reduces risk, but CLI execution still deserves review.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Content Sent to Remote Model API
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow posts image and mask references to RunComfy for model processing and downloads generated results. Users should avoid sending confidential or regulated images without approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The remote processing flow and endpoint allowlist are described by the skill. This confirms outbound processing, not malicious exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential File Mentioned in Home Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill names a token file in the user configuration directory. Secure permissions are documented, but users should rotate tokens if the host is shared or compromised.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credential location is explicit and the documented permissions are reasonable. The residual risk depends on local host security.

content.user_title: Edit Masked Image Regions with RunComfy
content.value_statement: Precise image edits often need masks, model routing, and careful prompt settings. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through RunComfy inpainting workflows.
actual_capabilities: Routes masked edits to Z-Image Turbo Inpainting on RunComfy.
Uses image and mask URLs with prompt, strength, and control-scale settings.
Falls back to description-based edit models when no mask is available.
Explains model choices for cleanup, replacement, and style-locked inpainting.
Documents RunComfy CLI invocation, output directories, and exit codes.
Provides privacy notes for remote processing, token storage, and trusted URLs.
limitations: It requires the external RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
Masked inpainting requires hosted image and mask URLs, not only local files.
It does not create masks; users need an upstream mask creation tool.
Results depend on remote model availability, rate limits, and input quality.

use_cases.title: Clean Product Photos
use_cases.description: Remove distractions or replace masked backgrounds while preserving the product shape, lighting, and brand presentation.

use_cases.title: Retouch Local Photo Regions
use_cases.description: Repair blemishes, gaps, or unwanted objects in a selected area without changing the rest of the image.

use_cases.title: Prepare Model-Specific Edit Jobs
use_cases.description: Choose a RunComfy model, build the inpainting request, and route no-mask edits to a fallback model.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Object Cleanup
prompt_templates.prompt: Inpaint this image using my image URL and mask URL. Remove the masked object and preserve the surrounding texture and lighting.

prompt_templates.title: Background Replacement
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the background mask to replace only the backdrop with a clean studio surface. Keep the product unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: No-Mask Local Edit
prompt_templates.prompt: I do not have a mask. Use the best description-based edit model to remove the object in the upper right.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Model Routing
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the inpainting and fallback routes for this edit, then choose strength and control-scale values for a realistic result.

output_examples.input: A product photo has a background mask and needs a plain gray studio backdrop.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects Z-Image Turbo Inpainting, recommends high strength, and preserves the product outside the mask.

output_examples.input: A street photo has a mask over overhead cables.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares a low-strength cleanup request that fills the sky and keeps rooflines intact.

output_examples.input: A user wants a local edit but does not have a mask.
output_examples.output: The assistant routes the task to a description-based edit model and asks for precise spatial instructions.

best_practices: Use only image and mask URLs that the user explicitly provides for this edit.
Describe what must stay unchanged outside the mask.
Start with lower strength for cleanup and increase strength for full replacement.
anti_patterns: Do not send confidential, regulated, or third-party images without permission.
Do not remove ownership marks or rights notices unless the user has authority.
Do not reuse a mask from a different image without checking alignment.

faq.question: Does this skill generate masks?
faq.answer: No. It consumes a mask URL. Create the mask with another tool before using masked inpainting.

faq.question: What service processes the images?
faq.answer: The skill uses the RunComfy CLI and RunComfy model APIs for remote image processing.

faq.question: Can it work without a mask?
faq.answer: Yes. It routes no-mask requests to description-based edit models when spatial instructions are clear.

faq.question: What inputs are required for masked inpainting?
faq.answer: A source image URL, a grayscale mask URL, and a prompt that describes the desired fill.

faq.question: Is it safe for private images?
faq.answer: Use caution. Image and mask references are sent to a third-party service for processing.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
